nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in lagoons and outer reef slopes between roughly 3 and 20 m depth, where individuals associate closely with host anemones.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Western Pacific reef; sheltered lagoons and outer reef edges with hard substrate hosting Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
host anemone optional (Heteractis crispa or Stichodactyla mertensii)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, marine pellet/flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended; small portions two to three times daily tend to work well.

avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
predatory fish that may eat small clownfish
triggers and large angelfish that may bully them

summary: The Orange Skunk Clownfish (Amphiprion sandaracinos) is a peaceful anemonefish from the Western Pacific, widely considered one of the calmer clownfish species. Generally regarded as reef-safe, it associates most closely with Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii anemones.
careNotes: The Orange Skunk Clownfish (Amphiprion sandaracinos) is widely regarded as one of the more peaceful clownfish species and is generally considered approachable enough for newer marine aquarists, though care ratings vary — some sources place it at Moderate rather than Easy because it can be out-competed at feeding time by bolder tankmates. In the wild it associates almost exclusively with Heteractis crispa and Stichodactyla mertensii anemones, but it does not require a host anemone in captivity and will readily shelter in rockwork instead. Like all clownfish, it is a protandrous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning individuals are born male and the dominant fish in a pair transitions to female. In a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male; sex change has been reported at around 5.1 cm total length. It is best kept singly or as a mated pair, as multiple individuals of this or any other clownfish species in the same tank tend to lead to serious aggression — the usual exception being tank-bred fish of the same species introduced together at a young age. While the species is generally regarded as peaceful toward unrelated tankmates, conspecific aggression is considered high.
breedingNotes: The Orange Skunk Clownfish has been bred and reared in captivity. Like other clownfish, it is an egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs (eggs that sink and adhere to a flat surface) on a cleaned surface near the host anemone. The male guards and aerates the clutch, which generally hatches in roughly 6 to 11 days depending on water temperature. Larvae are extremely small at hatching and require very small live foods — rotifers first, then enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp — in a dedicated rearing tank. The species is generally reported as somewhat harder to spawn than the Common Clownfish (Amphiprion ocellaris) or the Percula Clownfish (A. percula), though easier than the Pink Skunk Clownfish (A. perideraion).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Allen, 1972), family (Pomacentridae), max size (14 cm TL), preferred wild temperature (27-29.3°C), Western Pacific distribution, protandrous hermaphroditism, length at sex change (5.1 cm TL), and host anemones (Heteractis crispa, Stichodactyla mertensii).

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply care guide
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of 30 US gal minimum tank, 75-82°F temperature, 8.1-8.4 pH, 1.020-1.025 SG, peaceful temperament, omnivorous diet, and reef-safe behaviour.

additional.name: AquariumDomain species profile
additional.notes: Used to corroborate the peaceful temperament and reef-safe rating where LiveAquaria's 'semi-aggressive' tag disagreed with most other community sources.

fish.breedingNotes: The Orange Skunk Clownfish has been produced at commercial scale in captivity, though it is generally reported as somewhat harder to spawn than the Common Clownfish (Amphiprion ocellaris) or the Percula Clownfish (A. percula). Larvae require rotifer cultures and enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
